name: What Lazarus Becomes
description: The fresh Hollow's warning — not finished, still building, stop them — maps directly to what Kehl has been sensing from inside the Lazarus network. Kell believes Kehl can confirm the connection between the ancient Hollow and the modified compound, from the only perspective that matters: the inside.
accept_text: Kell sits with it for a long time. 'Kehl,' he says finally. 'The sergeant at the windward approach. He can feel the network from inside it. Forty-three subjects. The coherence. The fact that someone designed the coordination, that it didn't emerge naturally from Stone's original compound.' He looks up. 'If the Hollow in the lair are the ancient version of the same process — and I believe they are — then Kehl is positioned at an earlier stage of the same transformation. He can describe it from the inside while he still has the language for it.' He stands. 'Ask him about the Hollow specifically. Ask him what the network feels like at high integration, what the endpoint is. He's been watching the ancient ones from a distance for two days from high ground. I think he knows what he's looking at.' A pause. 'And ask him how long he has. I need to know if we have time to act on any of this.'
complete_text: Kehl listens to everything without turning from the ridge. When you finish describing the vassal's warning, he nods slowly, the nod of a soldier confirming intelligence he already had but needed verified. 'I know what they are,' he says. 'The network doesn't distinguish between what I am and what they've been for centuries. They're present in it — very quiet, very deep, but present.' He looks at his hands. 'The modification I received was designed to accelerate integration. Not to preserve the possibility of reversal. Whoever modified Stone's compound knew what the endpoint was and built toward it deliberately.' He looks north at the Aetherian crystal formations. 'The Elder People chose to stop. All of them, simultaneously, from inside something much larger than what exists now. Whatever they built — they had enough of themselves left to make that choice together.' He pauses. 'I've been sitting here two days thinking about whether I still have enough of myself left to make that choice.' He doesn't answer his own question. 'Go back to Kell. Tell him what I said. Tell him the Abbot needs to hear this too.'
